inlineContent.text: Onboarding wizards, multi-step forms, guided checkout. They all share two awkward facts: each step needs to see what previous steps collected, and “done” means tearing down every screen the sequence pushed.
Solving that with raw
and a chain of
hand-offs forces the state to live somewhere uncomfortable. Usually that’s in a parent view or a view model that outlives the sequence and has no business knowing about it. Drop in an
and you’ve added a shared mutable container to the environment for a workflow that only existed for thirty seconds.
collapses both problems into one type. A struct that owns the data the sequence collects, and also which also uses that knowledge to determine which step comes next.
This makes the flow state dependent.
Just like SwiftUI wants.
The protocol is small. An associated
, a checkpoint property that Navigator manages for you, and two methods that drive the sequence forward.
The three lifecycle hooks have empty default implementations. Override only the ones you care about.
Here’s a real flow: an onboarding sequence that collects a name and email, submits them, and shows a confirmation screen before tearing down.

code: private let handler: @Sendable (String, String, String) async throws -> Void
init(handler: @escaping @Sendable (String, String, String) async throws -> Void) {

inlineContent.text: Notice what isn’t there. No
. No class. No
. No shared store. The flow is a plain struct. The flow
is
its state.
That’s the part worth repeating. The flow struct holds the data the sequence collects, and it travels through the destination enum as an associated value.
Each step receives the current flow, mutates its own fields, and hands the updated copy back to the navigator.
There’s no view model. There’s no shared store. The
binds straight into the flow’s stored properties via
‘s projected binding, and the button hands the mutated copy back to the navigator. That’s the entire data layer for this screen.

Value semantics are doing real work here. Each pushed step gets its own copy of the flow as it existed at push time, so navigating back doesn’t observe later mutations, and SwiftUI’s diff over the navigation path behaves exactly the way it does for any other
value. The flow is just data in the path.
Two methods on
move a flow forward.
stamps a checkpoint capturing the current navigator and its path index, then calls the flow’s
and routes the result.
calls
and routes the result.
Both methods route the returned
the same way. A
pushes the next step. A
, or
returns the stack to the checkpoint and fires the corresponding hook.
The flow’s
is the state machine. It inspects the current fields and decides what comes next or that you’re done.
Each branch reads a piece of state and decides what comes next. Notice that
never returns
here. The flow’s last screen always pushes a confirmation view, and terminating the flow is the confirmation view’s job, not the state machine’s. Past five or six steps this chain of
-checks turns into a debugging puzzle and an explicit step enum is the right refactor.
The flow needs to do
something
with the data it collects. In the example above, the initializer takes an async closure and stores it privately.

code: private let handler: @Sendable (String, String, String) async throws -> Void

inlineContent.text: That’s how the flow gets the thing it eventually needs to call without coupling its definition to a specific submission service. The closure is
, set once at construction, called from the flow’s own
method.
Keep the handler private. Views inside the flow have no reason to know how submission works, only that they can ask the flow to perform it. The flow exposes a method:
The method is non-mutating on purpose. A
method can’t be called against actor-isolated
storage in Swift 6 — the compiler refuses to hand the storage out as
across the suspension. Keeping
non-mutating sidesteps the problem and matches the natural call site
There’s also a small price on conformance. Closures are not
, so the moment you add one, the auto-synthesized
conformance disappears. Implement it manually over the value properties and ignore the closure.

code: static func == (lhs: OnboardingFlow, rhs: OnboardingFlow) -> Bool {

inlineContent.text: This is safe because the closure is fixed for the lifetime of the flow. Excluding it from equality cannot make two genuinely-different flows compare equal in any way navigation cares about.
The flow protocol is synchronous on purpose.
and
are decisions, not effects. Anything that needs to await belongs in the view layer, which calls
once the await resolves.
Notice what the view doesn’t know. It doesn’t know the flow holds a closure. It doesn’t know what
does internally. It asks the flow to submit, and on success hands the same flow back to the navigator. The flow’s internals stay the flow’s business.
On failure the view stays put and surfaces the error. On success,
pushes the confirmation screen. The confirmation screen terminates the flow explicitly, which is the next section.
If an awaited failure should kill the flow entirely rather than let the user retry in place, call
instead of
. The flow’s
hook fires after the stack has already returned to the checkpoint, so use it for telemetry or for surfacing a toast at the entry point, not for recovery inside the flow.
A flow can mix presentation methods across its steps. The first step opens as a sheet, the rest push within it.
The checkpoint is recorded relative to the navigator that called
, so completion dismisses the sheet
and
pops back to whatever pushed the start button. One mechanism, no manual coordination between push depth and sheet presentation.
See
for the full picture of how Navigator tears down nested presentations.
A flow ends one of two ways. Either
returns one of the terminal
cases (
), or a view calls the equivalent method on the navigator directly (
). Both routes do the same thing: restore the navigation stack to the checkpoint, then call the matching hook on the flow.
The onboarding example uses the imperative form. The confirmation screen knows it’s the last step, so it doesn’t need the state machine to figure that out.

code: Text("That's all there is to it!")

inlineContent.text: The hooks themselves have empty default implementations.
Override only what you need. A welcome toast in
. Telemetry in
. Crash reporting in
The hooks fire
after
the navigator has returned to the checkpoint, not before. If

wants to show a confirmation, the user is already back at the entry point, not on the final step of the flow. That’s almost always what you want, but it’s worth being explicit about so you don’t try to push something onto a stack that no longer exists.

The example above is a plain struct. For most sequences that’s all you need. Three or four fields, value semantics throughout, the navigator copies the flow from step to step and nobody else has to think about it.

That stops being a good fit when the flow holds state that’s expensive to copy or state that has to be shared with code outside the flow. A checkout sequence accumulating cart line items, a shipping address, a payment method, promo codes, and tax calculations is a lot of bytes to hand around every time the user taps Next. It probably also needs to coordinate with a global cart that’s already a reference type.
In which case we simply make the flow a class.

code: func next() -> FlowResult<Destinations> { /* inspect state, return next step */ }
static func == (lhs: CheckoutFlow, rhs: CheckoutFlow) -> Bool { lhs === rhs }
func hash(into hasher: inout Hasher) { hasher.combine(ObjectIdentifier(self)) }

inlineContent.text: is by identity now, which is the right semantic for a class. Two instances are the same flow only if they’re literally the same object.
is no longer
either, because the class itself is the mutable storage.
We can go further.

is a protocol, so flows themselves can be protocols. That’s how you reuse a screen across different sequences. An “address entry” view that belongs in both checkout and profile editing doesn’t want to be hard-coded to one concrete flow type.
The view is generic over any flow that satisfies the requirement. Both
and
route their address step through the same implementation. Each flow still declares its own
enum and decides which step comes when. Only the screen is shared.

content.text: When Not to Use a Flow

inlineContent.text: A two-screen interaction that just hands a value back doesn’t justify a flow. A named checkpoint with a typed return value is lighter and more direct. See

Reach for a flow when the sequence accumulates state across three or more steps, when the same sequence needs to start from multiple entry points in the app, or when the sequence ends in an effect that needs the accumulated state in one place.
